studies.title: SSH Brute Force Detection and Active Response

timeline.event: Wazuh Rule 5551 fires — 5 failed SSH logins from 10.0.0.5 within 60 seconds
Wazuh active response triggers — blocks source IP via iptables on endpoint
Alert forwarded to Telegram via daily-digest workflow
Manual review: source identified as external scan (not Kali red-team node)
pfSense rule added to block entire /24 subnet permanently
Wazuh threshold tuned: alert at 3 failures instead of 5 for non-internal IPs

studies.detection: Wazuh authentication rule 5551 with active response script configured to call iptables. Alert threshold: 5 failures in 60 seconds from same source IP.
studies.mitre: T1110.001 — Brute Force: Password Guessing. Adversary attempting to gain access to SSH service via credential stuffing.
studies.mitigation: Active response auto-block deployed. pfSense subnet rule added. Threshold reduced for external sources. SSH port-knock considered as future hardening.
studies.lessons: Active response is powerful but must be tuned carefully — a misconfigured threshold can block legitimate users. Always verify source IP before making permanent firewall rules.

studies.title: False Positive Tuning — PowerShell via Sysmon EventID 1

timeline.event: Sysmon EventID 1 fires — PowerShell.exe spawned by svchost.exe with encoded command
Wazuh rule 92201 triggers — Suspicious PowerShell execution detected
Alert reviewed: parent process is Windows Update service (svchost.exe -k netsvcs)
Process hash verified against known-good Windows Update helper
Wazuh exclusion rule added: whitelist specific parent process hash + command pattern

studies.detection: Sysmon EventID 1 (Process Create) with CommandLine containing Base64 encoded PowerShell (-EncodedCommand). Wazuh rule 92201 matches suspicious PS patterns.
studies.mitre: T1059.001 — Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell. Legitimate use by Windows Update, misidentified as adversary technique.
studies.mitigation: Exclusion rule added with triple condition: parent process name + parent process hash + command substring. Avoids broad whitelisting that could mask real attacks.
studies.lessons: Never exclude by process name alone — always combine with hash or full path. Document every exclusion rule with the reason in a comment so future analysts understand the context.

studies.title: Authorized Port Scan Detection — Red Team Exercise

timeline.event: Suricata rule ET SCAN fires — Nmap SYN scan detected from 192.168.10.5 (Kali node)
Alert forwarded to Wazuh via UNIX socket
Wazuh Rule 40101 fires — Port Scan Detected
Cross-reference internal IP list — 192.168.10.5 is the authorized Kali lab node
Alert confirmed as authorized red-team activity
Suricata rule updated — tag internal scans as INFO not ALERT when source is lab VLAN

studies.detection: Suricata ET SCAN Nmap Scripting Engine signature (SID 2010937). Triggered on TCP SYN packets to multiple ports in rapid succession.
studies.mitre: T1046 — Network Service Discovery. Red team node performing authorized service enumeration against lab targets.
studies.mitigation: Created Suricata suppress rule for lab VLAN (192.168.10.0/24) to reduce noise during authorized tests. Documented authorized scan schedule in lab runbook.
studies.lessons: Red team exercises generate massive alert noise. Pre-authorise with signed scope document and suppress rules — but keep logs for post-exercise review. Never delete logs from authorized tests.
